document.title: Command Deck Engineering Dictionary
document.purpose: Provide a compact semantic dictionary of concepts related to Command Deck Engineering to LLMs, and to an interested public.

reader-orientation.purpose: This document is a dictionary: a compact semantic dictionary of recurring concepts used in Command Deck Engineering discussions, intended to provide quick grounding rather than full specifications.
reader-orientation.audience: The general public, Wing-Cat, and other LLMs assisting with Command Deck Engineering concepts.
design-goals: Provide short orientation anchors for Command Deck Engineering concepts.
Avoid redundancy with full specs or manifestos; definitions should be minimal but high-signal.
Entries include only facets meaningful to the concept rather than a rigid universal schema.

naming-convention.reason: Keys use kebab-case to visually distinguish semantic metadata from code identifiers or runtime attributes.

interpretation-guidance: Treat entries as conceptual orientation anchors rather than exhaustive doctrine.
Infer relationships contextually rather than assuming strict schema completeness.

required-fields.id: Canonical identifier of the concept; must match the key used in the dictionary.

name-tag.tags: List of classification tags describing the concept.
name-tag.hook: Short one-sentence orientation anchor (mental entry point).

typical-fields.origin: Conceptual provenance indicating how or where the idea emerged.
typical-fields.outline: Short list describing key structural aspects of the concept.
typical-fields.related-entries: List of concept ids referencing other dictionary entries.
typical-fields.name-tag.alias: List of aliases; an optional field within the name-tag.

name-tag.title: Command Deck Engineering
name-tag.hook: a software development methodology that organizes engineers, tools, and information flows around a command-deck-style environment to maintain intense focus while sharing situational awareness during the rapid construction of complex systems

command-deck-engineering.origin: Concept developed by Lion Kimbro through discussions with Wing-Cat, inspired by Artemis starship bridge simulator, NASA mission control, and the surgical team model from the Mythical Man Month.
outline: Software is developed in a shared operational environment, resembling a starship's bridge.
Everybody has a role.
Everybody is involved simultaneously in the development of the software.
The system being built is continuously visible to everybody.
The environment integrates human and computational analysis.
design-intent: Absolutely minimize downtime, maintain laser focus and excitement in a live development environment for making software.
Amplify command through distributed cognition -- Individual roles contribute to a larger synergy.
Take advantage of the various diverse skills and proclivities that people have, in a way that adds up magnificently.
Write software systems faster, more comprehensively, with fewer bugs than ever before, with great joy and social engagement in a meaningful and serious task.
Make visualization a first-rate actor in software development.
Take advantage of everything we know about the importance of continuous testing in software development.
operational-principles: A coordinated crew, building the system together.

conceptual-pillars.name: augmented-intellect
conceptual-pillars.description: computing systems should amplify human reasoning and collaborative problem solving

conceptual-pillars.name: visual-reasoning
conceptual-pillars.description: complex systems are understood best when their structure and relationships are represented visually

conceptual-pillars.name: operational-coordination
conceptual-pillars.description: teams managing complex systems benefit from shared situational awareness and coordinated specialized roles

name-tag.title: Command Deck Doctrin
name-tag.hook: the philosophical foundation of Command Deck Engineering, describing why software development benefits from a coordinated command environment rather than solitary programming

outline: Many people experience the act of building meaningful systems as a collective activity. Coordinated crew environments can unlock energy, focus, and engagement that solitary work often does not.
Engineering environments should foster shared purpose, visible impact, and meaningful roles. Human energy and motivation are important engineering resources.
Software systems can often be understood by a single architect, but constructing them requires distributed labor and high-bandwidth communication.

Shared attention dramatically increases coordination bandwidth. When a team focuses on the same artifact at the same time, misunderstandings are caught quickly, decisions propagate immediately, and the system model across participants remains synchronized.
Visual information mapping dramatically reduces cognitive load.
Visualization and shared displays allow the crew to maintain a single evolving picture of the system being built.
Engineering environments should allow the crew’s shared attention surface to be reshaped dynamically so that the team can focus collectively on the parts of the system that currently matter.
Complex engineering contains moments of real decision and consequence. Command Deck environments allow teams to confront those moments together.
When multiple people contribute to a system, preserving a coherent design becomes a central coordination challenge.
Shared situational awareness improves engineering coordination and reduces misunderstanding.
System structure should be externalized visually so that all participants can understand the architecture of the system.
System behavior should be continuously observable so that participants can see what the system is doing.
Human cognitive labor can be distributed into specialized roles that cooperate in real time, similar to operational environments such as ship bridges or mission control.

name-tag.title: Command Deck Lineage
name-tag.hook: the historical lineage of thinkers, systems, and operational environments that influenced the development of Command Deck Engineering

contributions: Man–Computer Symbiosis: the concept that humans and computers should form cooperative systems where each complements the strengths of the other.
Interactive computing: early advocacy for real-time interaction with computers rather than batch processing.
Networked information communities: vision of globally connected computers enabling collaborative work and shared knowledge.
Early leadership of ARPA's Information Processing Techniques Office (IPTO), which funded foundational research in interactive computing, networking, and time-sharing systems.
Conceptual foundation for later work by Douglas Engelbart, Alan Kay, and other pioneers of human-centered computing.
Chief Programmer Team model: organizing software development around a central architect supported by specialized roles such as backup programmer, tester, and librarian
Augmenting Human Intellect: computing systems designed to amplify human reasoning and collaboration
Collective IQ and shared interactive workspaces
Real-time collaborative environments for complex problem solving
Conceptual Integrity principle: coherent system design requires unified architectural vision
The surgical team model described in The Mythical Man-Month
Sketchpad: demonstration that complex systems can be designed and understood through interactive visual representations
Object-oriented computing and the Smalltalk environment: a live programming system where programs, objects, and user interfaces can be inspected and modified interactively.
Computing as a medium for thought: the idea that computers should support human understanding, learning, and creativity rather than merely executing procedures.
Dynamic, exploratory programming environments where systems remain visible and modifiable during execution.
Personal dynamic media: computing environments designed to amplify human thinking and communication.
Early vision of networked collaborative computing environments for groups of people working with shared information systems.
Information Mapping: a structured method for organizing complex information so that humans can understand and navigate it effectively.
Visual Language: development of visual methods for representing complex systems, policies, and arguments.
Mapping complex knowledge domains through diagrams and structured visual representations.
Advocated visual thinking tools to improve collective reasoning and decision making in organizations.
Operational control rooms where teams maintain shared situational awareness while managing complex systems in real time

augmenting-human-intellect.work: Augmenting Human Intellect: A Conceptual Framework
augmenting-human-intellect.author: Douglas Engelbart
contributions: Proposed that computers should augment human intellectual capability rather than merely automate calculations.
Introduced the concept of interactive computing environments designed to support complex problem solving.
Outlined systems for collaborative knowledge work, including shared documents, structured linking, and interactive displays.
Presented the idea that improving tools for thinking could improve humanity’s ability to solve large-scale problems.
Provided the conceptual foundation for later systems such as NLS (oN-Line System) and the famous 1968 'Mother of All Demos'.
Collaborative time-sharing computing environment where multiple engineers worked simultaneously on a shared system.
Culture of real-time collaborative development with immediate experimentation and system modification.
Shared awareness of system state and activity across engineers working on the same machine.
Demonstrated highly social software development centered around a shared computational environment.
Influenced later computing cultures including Lisp development environments and early open-source collaboration.

nls-system.organization: Augmentation Research Center, Stanford Research Institute
contributions: Interactive computing environment designed to augment human intellect and collaborative reasoning.
Supported real-time editing, linking, and navigation of complex knowledge structures.
Enabled shared displays and collaborative work across remote terminals.
Demonstrated computers as environments for coordinated human problem solving rather than isolated computation.
Introduced many concepts later seen in modern development environments: hypertext, version control, collaborative editing, and structured knowledge navigation.
Demonstrated the NLS (oN-Line System), showing interactive computing, hypertext linking, and real-time document editing.
Presented collaborative computing through live video links, allowing multiple engineers to work on shared information simultaneously.
Showed early forms of shared screens, remote collaboration, and visual navigation of complex information structures.
Illustrated computing as a medium for coordinated human thinking rather than passive calculation.
Visible computing: program structure and behavior should be directly visible to human perception
Immediate feedback computing: systems should show the consequences of changes instantly
Social cognition with computers: computing environments should support groups of people thinking together around shared artifacts

complete-map-of-every-thought.work: How to Make a Complete Map of Every Thought You Think
contributions: A conceptual framework for mapping human thought structures using explicit visual representations.
Explores how complex reasoning can be externalized into navigable cognitive maps.
Extends visual reasoning traditions such as information mapping and argument mapping.

artemis-bridge-simulator.creator: Thomas Robertson
contributions: Demonstrates distributed human roles cooperating through shared displays and coordinated attention
Illustrates attention management, role specialization, and collective situational awareness
A methodology organizing software development around shared attention, visual system models, and coordinated engineering roles.
Synthesizes ideas from augmented intellect systems, visual language, operational control rooms, and collaborative computing.

name-tag.title: Command Deck Systems Architecture
name-tag.hook: the physical and software infrastructure required to operate a Command Deck Engineering environment

outline: Bridge workspace layout with shared displays.
Ship computer systems that analyzes, visualizes, and manipulates code, and supports each person in their role.
Integrated development tools and telemetry.
Communication infrastructure linking bridge roles and support decks.

name-tag.title: Command Deck Protocols
name-tag.hook: the operational practices and communication procedures used by teams operating a command deck

outline: Role-specific communication channels.
Structured short command phrases for information retrieval.
Interrupt rules to maintain cognitive flow.
Situational reporting procedures.
Developed socially.

name-tag.title: Captain
name-tag.hook: the chief programmer responsible for directing implementation

outline: Defines architecture and system direction.
Approves structural decisions.
Coordinates work of bridge roles.
May write or direct implementation of code.

name-tag.title: First Officer
name-tag.hook: the backup programmer who maintains a parallel mental model of the system and verifies decisions

outline: Monitors logic and architecture decisions.
Challenges assumptions.
Steps in for captain when needed.

name-tag.title: Navigator
name-tag.hook: the officer responsible for tracking mission progress and maintaining awareness of project state

outline: Tracks mission objectives
Tracks what is working.
Tracks what is broken.
Tracks possible next steps.
Maintains a mission log of completed work.
Visualizes progress and possibilities.

name-tag.title: Operator
name-tag.hook: the bridge officer responsible for executing commands, orchestrating AI tools, and performing operational actions on the codebase

outline: Executes commands issued by the captain or bridge crew.
Operates AI systems and automation tools.
Performs bulk or system-wide operations on the codebase.
Handles mechanical development tasks to keep the captain focused on design and reasoning.

name-tag.title: Lead Visualizer
name-tag.hook: the bridge officer responsible for translating spoken design ideas into immediate diagrams of system structure

outline: Produces quick sketches of architecture.
Works closely with the captain to externalize the system mental model.
Feeds structural information to the visualization team.

name-tag.title: Visualization Team
name-tag.hook: a group responsible for refining architectural diagrams and maintaining the system visual map

outline: Converts rough sketches into formal diagrams.
Maintains architecture and flow maps.
Supports bridge displays with updated system visuals.

name-tag.title: Librarian
name-tag.hook: the officer responsible for retrieving artifacts and projecting them onto shared bridge displays

outline: Finds relevant documents, specs, code, or diagrams.
Projects artifacts to shared displays instantly.
Maintains organized knowledge repositories.

name-tag.title: Tool Developers
name-tag.hook: engineers responsible for building and maintaining the tools and computational systems used by the command deck

outline: Develop and maintain the ship computer and analysis systems.
Create visualization and telemetry tools used on the command deck.
Build automation systems that amplify the effectiveness of bridge roles.
Improve development infrastructure based on needs discovered during command deck sessions.

name-tag.title: Ship Computer
name-tag.hook: a computational system that continuously analyzes the codebase and provides queries, telemetry, and visualizations to the bridge

outline: Indexes and analyzes the codebase.
Runs tests and simulations.
Generates system diagrams and telemetry.
Answers queries from the bridge crew.

name-tag.title: Situation Display
name-tag.hook: the shared visual surface where the current state of the system, architecture, and mission progress are displayed to the command deck crew

outline: Displays system architecture, telemetry, and operational state.
Provides a shared reference point for the entire crew.
Allows rapid switching between different views such as architecture diagrams, execution traces, or strategic project maps.
Supports collective reasoning and decision-making.
